title: Energy Security
description: Because most of the world's oil and natural gas resources are concentrated in a small number of countries, many nations have economic, social, and geopolitical concerns about energy dependence. RAND research explores options for improving energy security—e.g., the use of domestically available alternative or renewable energy sources.
text:
Venezuela After Maduro: Q&A with RAND Experts
Many questions remain one month after the dramatic U.S. operation that captured Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro. How will sudden change reshape Venezuela's future? What are the regional impacts? And how might the U.S.-Venezuela relationship evolve?
Securing South Korea's Critical Minerals Supply Chains Through Trilateral Cooperation: Analysis and Tools for Supply Chain Net Assessment, Supply Chain Cooperation, and Economic Security
This report offers analysis, tools, and recommendations to strengthen trilateral cooperation and collaboration among South Korea, Japan, and the United States on critical minerals supply chains and economic security.
Undersea Cables Are Vulnerable to Sabotage—but This Takes Skill and Specialist Equipment
Countries have come to rely on a network of cables and pipes under the sea for their energy and communications. A coordinated attack on this network could have devastating consequences. But that is difficult business in a very challenging environment.
Protecting Europe's Critical Undersea Infrastructure Depends on Coordination and Collaboration
In May, the Polish navy intercepted a suspicious vessel linked to Russia's 'shadow fleet' near a critical power cable. This incident highlights the vulnerability of Europe's undersea infrastructure. Protecting this vital network requires coordinated efforts from national navies, government departments, and private companies.
Undersea Cables: Evidence submission by RAND Europe
This document provides RAND Europe's submission of evidence in relation to the UK Joint Committee on National Security Strategy's Call for Evidence on Undersea Cables.
Evolving threats to critical undersea infrastructure: Implications for European security and resilience
This Perspective examines the vulnerability of critical undersea infrastructure (CUI) to threats. It explores the growing dependency on CUI and the implications for European nations, the EU, and NATO. It suggests further policy action to protect CUI.
Seabed Safety, Security, and Stewardship: Future Implications for U.S. Coast Guard Strategy, Policy, and Operations
This report outlines the potential implications of seabed developments for the future U.S. Coast Guard. The Coast Guard might consider developing organizational structures, doctrine, partnerships, technology, and enhanced maritime domain awareness.
AI and the Future of the U.S. Electric Grid
AI data centers suck up energy like small cities. But AI could be a big part of the solution, too. There are risks here and grid operators need to move with caution. But AI could usher in an energy future that is more resilient, more efficient, and more affordable for customers.
Identifying Resilient, Sustainable Cooling Strategies for Los Angeles: How Might Landlords of Single-Family Homes Meet Indoor Temperature Thresholds?
In this report, the authors consider how the County of Los Angeles might develop its maximum indoor temperature threshold ordinance for single-family rental houses across various types of active and passive cooling.
